narrative: I'm sorry to share this tough news, but the law school's admission model predicted an 80% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on a careful look at your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.10—well below the typical 3.21 average—your LSAT score of 28 compared to the usual 36.7, and your placement in the bottom decile for both your first-year and third-year school grades, far from the middle-of-the-pack averages around 5.5 to 5.7. Your lower-middle family income also played a role, sitting below the dataset's average bracket, while factors like being male, Asian, and planning full-time studies aligned more closely with the majority of students. When the model weighed everything, your third-year decile ranking had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, followed closely by your LSAT score, then your first-year decile, family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and finally gender as the least influential. Each of these top factors nudged the prediction toward failure rather than success, with your low academic deciles and test scores standing out as particularly mismatched against students who typically pass the bar. In essence, your profile showed weaker academic signals compared to most applicants, who cluster around higher performances that signal bar exam readiness. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven flag that these areas deviated from successful patterns. Looking ahead, you could realistically retake the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above 36.7, as that's a key factor you can directly improve with focused preparation. Strengthening your overall academic record—perhaps through additional coursework or a post-baccalaureate program—might help shift those decile rankings in future applications. While family income is harder to change short-term, highlighting any scholarships, work ethic, or support systems in your story could contextualize it. Your gender and full-time plans are already in line with passers, so lean into those strengths. With targeted effort on grades, GPA boosters, and LSAT, you could realign your profile toward the successful averages and boost your odds significantly next time. Keep pushing—you've got this.
